3 Fundamentals of Alternating Current
Rationale
Why is it important for you to learn this skill?
Alternating current (ac) circuits are widely used as the means of distribution for electric
energy. Most of your work involves ac circuits. You were introduced to ac resistive
circuits in first period training. In second period training, you will learn about additional
ac circuit properties that are crucial to your understanding of the electrical theory and the
electrician trade.
Outcome
When you have completed this module, you will be able to
Describe the fundamentals of alternating current (ac).
Objectives
1. Describe the generation of an ac sine wave.
2. Determine the output frequency of an ac generator.
¢ 3. Calculate standard ac sine wave values.
Introduction
This module discusses the generation of an alternating current and shows how a graph
called a sine wave is generated. It also describes the relationship between output
frequency, poles, and rotational speed. Finally, the module explains effective (RMS)
voltage and performs sine wave calculations. This module will prepare you for further
exploration of ac circuits in second period theory.

Although the conductor velocity may be constant,
> e The angle at which the flux lines are cut constantly changes;
e The number of lines cut per second continuously changes; and
e Therefore, the instantaneous value of voltage induced continuously changes.
If the angle of cut is increased to the maximum, which is a cutting rate of 90°, the rate of
cutting of lines of magnetic force increases. If a conductor’s circular path is between the
two magnetic poles, the maximum or peak emf (Emax, Em, OF Epeak) is generated when the
conductor cuts across the field at right angles, as in Figure 2. |

The maximum emf is generated at two points on the 360° circular path: 90° and 270°.
As a conductor loop is rotated in the magnetic field of a generator, it passes by alternate
i) north and south magnetic poles. Each time the conductor loop passes a pole, one
alternation is completed. When it passes one pair of poles, it has completed two
alternations, or one electrical cycle, as depicted in Figure 3.

The production of usable electricity requires a generator. An electrical generator isa |
machine that has coils of conductors mounted on a shaft that is turning within magnetic
fields produced by several electromagnets. A generator that produces ac is often referred
to as an alternator. If the alternator coil rotates past one pair of magnetic poles (north and
south), then for every rotation of the generator shaft, one electrical cycle is completed. If
the alternator coil is spinning at 60 rotations per second, it produces 60 cycles per second, |
Regarding an ac generator, consider the following concepts about frequency. |
© Thenumber of electrical cycles completed in one second is the frequency of the |
output of the alternator.
The International System of Units (SI) symbol for frequency is f- |
e The unit of measurement for frequency is hertz, represented by the SI
symbol Hz. |
» The time required to complete one electrical cycle is known as the period
(time period). @ |
NOTE
You may see the term cycles per second, which was previously used as
the unit of measurement for frequency; 60 cycles per second is equal
to 60 Hz. |
|

pages.section: Objective Two - Frequency and Magnetic Poles
figures: Figure 5 - A four-pole machine produces one cycle as it rotates through 180 mechanical degrees
Figure 6 - A four-pole machine produces 720 electrical degrees as it rotates through 360 mechanical degrees
pages.content:
ee
Frequency and Magnetic Poles
r If an alternator has only one pair of magnetic poles (north and south) and has a shaft
speed of 15 revolutions per second, the number of completed cycles per second would
also be 15 cycles per second (Hz). In one revolution (360 mechanical degrees), the
waveform of a two-pole machine passes through one cycle (360 electrical degrees).
If the alternator has several electromagnets that produce several pairs of magnetic poles,
then for every rotation of the shaft, the coil of the conductor would pass by more than one
pair of poles. This machine completes more than one electrical cycle for each mechanical
rotation of the shaft. In any alternator, as a conductor completes a revolution, it passes
through 360 mechanical degrees. |
NOTE
Remember that 360 electrical degrees are produced when a conductor
passes by two opposite poles.
In Figure 5, only one half of a revolution of a four-pole machine has occurred (180
mechanical degrees), but because two opposite poles have been passed, 360 electrical
degrees (or one full cycle) is produced, as is illustrated in the waveform.
LN|
as +
Lee >
IN]
Figure 5 - A four-pole machine produces one cycle as it rotates
through 180 mechanical degrees.
One full revolution (360 mechanical degrees) of a four-pole machine produces
720 electrical degrees. Since one cycle is 360 electrical degrees, the waveform in
Figure 6 displays that two cycles have occurred.
LN|
IN|
Figure 6 - A four-pole machine produces 720 electrical degrees as it rotates
through 360 mechanical degrees.
Ifa four-pole alternator completes thirty revolutions per second, it would complete
60 cycles in one second. Two cycles per revolution x 30 revolutions per second equals
» 60 cycles per second or 60 Hz.

The output frequency of an ac generator or alternator is determined by two factors: |
© The number of poles (or pole pairs) and |
e The revolutions per minute of the driven shaft. |
The mechanical speed or rotational frequency of the machine is typically rated in |
revolutions per minute, which is how many times per minute the shaft of the machine
rotates 360 mechanical degrees. The abbreviation for revolutions per minute is r/min, |
although rpm is often still used in industry.
If the driven shaft of a two-pole machine is rotating at one revolution per second
(or 60 r/min), the output frequency is one cycle per second (or 1 Hz). The conductor |
passes two poles (one north and one south) in one second. If the shaft of a \
four-pole machine is rotating at the same rate, its output frequency is 2 Hz since two ))
cycles are completed in one second. j
NOTE
The rotation produced by a mechanical force, such as an engine or
turbine, is typically measured in r/min. Rotational frequency is more
technically correct, but speed is common in industry.
In either example, if the rate of rotation incr:

Calculating Frequency
‘ The frequency of an alternator is the product of the number of cycles completed in one
®) revolution multiplied by the number of revolutions completed in one second. Frequency
in hertz is equal to the number of completed cycles per revolution multiplied by the
revolutions per second.
The number of cycles can be expressed as pairs of poles since a conductor passing by one
pair of poles equals one cycle. Rotational speed is often expressed as revolutions per
minute rather than seconds or minutes divided by 60.
imcapenter inl number of pairs of poles x revolutions per minute
60
Since the information on the nameplate of machines typically indicates the number of
individual poles rather than pairs of poles, the formula is most often expressed as follows:
ERAW -
f 120
Where
¢ p=number of individual poles
¢ n=rotational frequency
The denominator is 120 because the number of individual poles (p) must be divided
by two to account for the conductor needing to pass two poles to make a complete
electrical cycle.

Instantaneous Values
“The values produced at any given instant in the cycle are called instantaneous values. {
) ‘The value of voltage induced at any angle can be found by multiplying the maximum emf
of the alternation by the sine of the angle at which the conductor is cutting the field.
This value of emf at a specific point in the cycle is called the instantaneous value of B
induced voltage and is represented by the lowercase symbol e.

Effective Values
An alternating voltage is constantly changing and reversing, and therefore the current in a
circuit supplied by an alternating voltage is also constantly changing and reversing. Since
the values are constantly changing, it is necessary to assign some value as a rating for
electrical equipment and circuits, By convention, a value is used that represents the same
heating effect in an ac circuit as that produced in a de circuit.
A resistive ac circuit labelled as 100 V ac produces the same amount of heat that a
100 V de circuit produces if it has the same resistance. This value of ac is called the
effective value. The effective value of an ac voltage may be 100 V, but the maximum
Voltage is higher.
This effective value is also referred to as the RMS value. When dealing with pure sine
waves, the effective value and the RMS value are equal. RMS (root mean square) refers
to the square root of the mean Squares of all the instantaneous values of voltage of the
| waveform. For the purposes of this module, you can assume that the RMS value is the
| RMS value of a pure sine or sinusoidal waveform. The effective value always takes place
at 45°. The same value can also be found at 135°, 225°, and 315°, which are all 45° from
the zero.
